items.q: What defines a pure public good?
items.correct: Non-rivalry (one person's use doesn't reduce others') and non-excludability (you can't stop people benefiting) — e.g. national defence, street lighting.
items.misconception: A public good is anything provided by the government.
items.random_wrong: A public good is anything that's free.
items.why: Government provides many private goods (e.g. NHS treatment is rival). The economic definition is about the GOOD's properties, not who provides it. Students confuse provider with technical category.

items.q: What is the technical definition of a recession?
items.correct: Two consecutive quarters of negative real GDP growth — a contraction of the economy.
items.misconception: A recession is when prices fall and people lose jobs.
items.random_wrong: A recession is when the stock market crashes.
items.why: Job losses and stock falls often accompany recessions but don't define them. The technical definition is GDP-based. Students conflate symptoms (unemployment) with definition (negative growth).

items.q: What does price elasticity of demand measure?
items.correct: The responsiveness of quantity demanded to a change in price — the percentage change in quantity divided by the percentage change in price.
items.misconception: PED measures how flexible or stretchy a product is.
items.random_wrong: PED measures whether a product is expensive or cheap.
items.why: Students take 'elasticity' literally. It is a measure of sensitivity — how much quantity responds when price changes. The product itself isn't 'elastic'; demand for it is.

items.q: At equilibrium, supply and demand are…
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Equal. “Both zero” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's Shortage. “Surplus” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: At £3, demand 200, supply 200. Equilibrium price, in £?
items.why: Exact answer: 3. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
